title: CONTAINER Act
plain_summary: The CONTAINER Act lets border states put up temporary movable barriers on federal land near the borders with Canada and Mexico. States can do this without asking for special permission first. The barriers can stay for up to one year to help with border security. If border security goals are not met, the barriers can be extended for additional 90-day periods.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Energy and Natural Resources.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Creating Obstructions Necessary To Address Illegal and Nefarious Entry Rapidly Act or the CONTAINER Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Placement of Movable, Temporary Structures on Certain Federal Land to Secure an International Border of the United States
sections.text: This section explains how border states can place temporary barriers on federal land near the borders. A border state means any state that touches the border with Canada (called the northern border) or the border with Mexico (called the southern border). Federal land means land owned and managed by the federal government that is close to these borders. This includes land managed by agencies like the Bureau of Indian Affairs, Bureau of Land Management, Bureau of Reclamation, Forest Service, US Fish and Wildlife Service, and National Park Service. Operational control means the government has effective control over border security as defined in an earlier law from two thousand six. The Secretary concerned means either the Secretary of the Interior or the Secretary of Agriculture, depending on which agency manages the specific federal land. Border states do not need to get special permission to place movable temporary structures on federal land for border security purposes. However, the state must give the appropriate federal secretary at least forty five days notice before placing the structure. These temporary structures can stay in place for up to one year. The time period can be extended for ninety day periods if the federal secretary approves the extension. Before approving an extension, the secretary must talk with the Commissioner of US Customs and Border Protection. The secretary must approve the extension if the Commissioner says that operational control of the border has not been achieved yet.
